ONGARO, ONGARO PC, FOR PLAINTIFF VICTORIA GOLUNOVA; MICAH NASH, OGLOZA FORTNEY + FRIEDMAN LLP, FOR PLAINTIFF MARIA RYCHKOVA; RONALD FOREMAN FOR DEFENDANTS ANN AND DANIEL AKHROMSTEV AND DANMA CONSULTING; OTHER PARTIES AND COUNSEL AS REFLECTED IN REPORTER'S TRANSCRIPT. HEARING IS HELD ON RECEIVER'S EX PARTE PETITION FOR INSTRUCTION. RULING: THE COURT HAVING REVIEWED THE PLEADINGS AND HAVING HEARD ORAL ARGUMENT RULES AS FOLLOWS: THE RECEIVER'S EX PARTE PETITION FOR INSTRUCTION IS GRANTED IN PART. RECEIVER IS ORDERED TO OVERSEE TRANSFER OF CURRENT CALIFORNIA HOME HEALTH LLC (CHH) PATIENTS TO OTHER LICENSED AND QUALIFIED HEALTH SERVICE PROVIDERS; TO TAKE STEPS NECESSARY TO ENSURE CONTINUED FINANCIAL VIABILITY OF CHH; PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO COOPERATE WITH RECEIVER. RECEIVER'S REQUEST FOR ORDER DIRECTING HIM TO TURN OVER POSSESSION OF CHH TO PLAINTIFFS IS DENIED. COURT ISSUES WRITTEN ORDER. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM. REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

MINUTES: EX PARTE HEARING ON FURTHER STATUS CONFERENCE: APPEARANCES (THROUGH COURT CALL): THOMAS A. SEAMAN, CFA, RECEIVER; DAVID R. ONGARO, ONGARO PC, FOR PLAINTIFF VICTORIA GOLUNOVA; MICAH NASH, OGLOZA FORTNEY + FRIEDMAN LLP, FOR PLAINTIFF MARIA RYCHKOVA; RONALD FOREMAN FOR DEFENDANTS ANN AND DANIEL AKHROMSTEV AND DANMA CONSULTING; OTHER PARTIES AS REFLECTED IN REPORTER'S TRANSCRIPT. FURTHER HEARING IS HELD ON STATUS OF IMPLEMENTATION OF COURT'S MARCH 27 ORDER. COURT EXPRESSES EXPECTATION THAT ALL PATIENTS WILL BE DISCHARGED OR TRANSFERRED TO OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS NO LATER THAN MAY 20. COURT DIRECTS RECEIVER TO COMPLY WITH ALL NECESSARY CONDITIONS SO AS TO PRESERVE POSSIBILITY THAT COMPANY WILL BE ABLE TO RETAIN RATHER THAN REFUND STIMULUS PAYMENT. COURT AUTHORIZES RECEIVER TO RETAIN DANMA CONSULTING OR OTHER BILLING COMPANY TO ASSIST WITH COLLECTION OF ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLE AFTER INVESTIGATING OPTIONS; PARTIES DIRECTED TO SUBMIT INFORMATION REGARDING INDEPENDENT THIRD-PARTY BILLING COMPANIES TO RECEIVER. PARTIES AGREE TO PROCEED WITH SCHEDULING OF VOLUNTARY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE, WITH APPROPRIATE INPUT/PARTICIPATION BY RECEIVER. PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO APPEAR FOR FURTHER STATUS CONFERENCE ON MAY 20 AT 11:00 A.M. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Jun-23-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT YELENA ASADOV'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Yelena Asadov's motion to quash is granted.

A plaintiff can rely on CCP 474 to substitute a party for a Doe defendant where the plaintiff is ignorant of the party's identity or facts giving rise to liability against that party. (See generally General Motors Corporation v. Superior Court (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 580.)

"The lack of knowledge of the true name of a defendant, however, must be 'real and not feigned.' [Citation.] A plaintiff must actually be ignorant of the facts giving him a cause of action against a defendant. 'Ignorance of the facts is the critical issue, and whether it be due to misinformation or negligence is not relevant.'" (Dover v Sadowinski (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 113, 116.) "Ignorance of the facts giving rise to a cause of action, like ignorance of the true name, should be real and not feigned. The pivotal question in this regard is "did plaintiff know facts?" not "did plaintiff know or believe that she had a cause of action based on those facts?" (Scherer v. Mark (1976) 64 Cal.App.3d 834, 841 (emphasis in original).)

In this case, plaintiff's judicial admissions show that they were not genuinely ignorant of Asadov's identity or role in the alleged wrongdoing. Plaintiff's allegations show that Asadov played a "critical role in the operational management" of CHH, she cut off plaintiff's managerial access, acted "confrontational and erratic" towards plaintiff, received a bonus under false pretenses, and lied about security issues. (Complaint, pars. 16-17, 19- 20, 22-26, 28, 30, 34 and 35.) The allegations clearly show that Asadov was acting in unison with defendant Daniel Akhromtsev. Most importantly, paragraph 28 of the Complaint alleges that Asadov set up a competing business with Ann Akhromtsev. This allegation belies plaintiff's assertion that plaintiff recently discovered the competing business during a deposition.

In sum, plaintiff's claim of genuine ignorance rings hollow. Plaintiff's judicial admissions show that she did not "lack[] knowledge of [Asadov's] connection with the case or with [plaintiff's] injuries." (General Motors Corporation v. Superior Court (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 580, 594.)

Counsel for Asadov is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Appointing Receiver And For Preliminary Injunction
Off Calendar Dec-23-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
David R. Ongaro, Esq. and Amelia D. Winchester, Esq. of ONGARO PC, T:415.433.3900, for plaintiff Golunova;
Micah Nash, Esq. of OGLIZA FORTNEY LLP, T:415.238.2119, for plaintiff Rychkova;
Ronald D. Foreman, Esq. of FOREMAN & BRASSO, T:415.433.3475, for defendants/cross-defendants.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFFs VICTORIA GOLUNOVA and MARIYA RYCHKOVA's Motion For Order Appointing Receiver And For Preliminary Injunction, as follows:

Plaintiffs' motion to appoint receiver and for a preliminary injunction is granted. Plaintiffs show that Daniel Akhromtsev has been using CHH's funds to make questionable expenditures, including monthly car payments, credit card payments, gasoline, grocery shopping, dining, food delivery, and other personal expenses; and generally that Defendants have been managing CHH without the full knowledge or consent of Plaintiffs, although CHH's operating agreement provides that it "shall be controlled by its Members, acting unanimously." Defendants summarily assert that the challenged payments constituted ordinary business expenses, but fail adequately to explain or substantiate that contention. Plaintiffs, who own 66% of CHH, are entitled to a receiver to protect their interests in that entity. (See Code Civ. Proc., 564(b)(9).) The court intends to request the receiver, as his first order of business, to conduct an independent audit of CHH and report his findings and recommendations back to the court. The court intends by that request to attempt to minimize the expense and burden on the parties and the business stemming from the appointment of a receiver. Plaintiffs should present at the hearing a modified proposed form of order incorporating that request.

Plaintiff to prepare and submit a proposed order.
